sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A physics instructor used tubes with one closed end to model resonance in an air column. The speed of sound was taken as 340 m/s. For tube lengths of 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, and 1.00 m, the fundamental frequencies were approximately 340, 170, 113, and 85 Hz. Students were reminded that a tube closed at one end has fundamental wavelength λ = 4L.
questions.stem: What is the approximate fundamental frequency for a 0.50 m closed tube?

explanations.D: Correct. Figure 1 shows 170 Hz at 0.50 m. It also follows from f = v/(4L) = 340/(2.0) = 170 Hz.
explanations.A: 85 Hz corresponds to a 1.00 m tube.
explanations.B: 340 Hz corresponds to a 0.25 m tube.
explanations.C: 680 Hz would be too high and ignores the closed-tube wavelength relationship.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Fundamental frequency of a tube closed at one end as length increases.

questions.stem: Why does frequency decrease as tube length increases?

choices.C: Longer tubes support longer wavelengths, and f = v/λ
choices.A: Longer tubes increase sound speed in air
choices.B: Frequency is directly proportional to length in closed tubes

explanations.C: Correct. For a closed tube, the fundamental wavelength is 4L. Increasing L increases wavelength, and with wave speed fixed, frequency decreases.
explanations.A: Sound speed is determined mainly by the medium and temperature, not tube length.
explanations.B: The graph and equation show inverse proportionality rather than direct proportionality.
explanations.D: Longer tubes still resonate; they resonate at lower frequencies.

questions.stem: What is the fundamental wavelength for the 0.75 m tube?

explanations.B: Correct. For a tube closed at one end, λ = 4L. With L = 0.75 m, λ = 3.0 m.
explanations.A: 0.75 m is the tube length, not the wavelength.
explanations.C: 1.5 m would correspond to 2L, not the closed-tube fundamental wavelength.
explanations.D: 4.0 m would be λ for a 1.0 m closed tube.

questions.stem: Which harmonics are present in an ideal tube closed at one end?

explanations.A: Correct. A tube closed at one end supports odd harmonics because one end is a displacement node and the other is an antinode. The allowed wavelengths follow that boundary condition.
explanations.B: Even harmonics are characteristic of different boundary conditions, not a one-closed-end tube.
explanations.C: All integer harmonics occur in an open-open or closed-closed ideal pipe, not an open-closed pipe.
explanations.D: Higher odd harmonics can occur, so it is not limited only to the fundamental.

questions.stem: If temperature increased and sound speed rose, what would happen to the tube’s fundamental frequency?

choices.B: It would remain fixed because length is fixed

explanations.D: Correct. For a fixed tube length, wavelength is set by boundary conditions. Since f = v/λ, increasing sound speed increases frequency.
explanations.A: Decreasing frequency would require lower wave speed or longer wavelength, neither of which is described.
explanations.B: Length fixes the resonant wavelength, but frequency still depends on sound speed.
explanations.C: A higher sound speed would not eliminate resonance.

questions.passage: A laser diffraction experiment used a single slit and a screen 2.0 m away. The laser wavelength was 600 nm. Students varied slit width and measured the distance from the central maximum to the first minimum. For slit widths of 0.10, 0.20, and 0.30 mm, the first-minimum distances were 12 mm, 6 mm, and 4 mm, respectively. Small-angle behavior was assumed.
questions.stem: What first-minimum distance is measured for the 0.20-mm slit?

explanations.C: Correct. Figure 2 shows 6 mm for the 0.20-mm slit. This is half the 0.10-mm value because diffraction width is inversely related to slit width.
explanations.D: 20 mm is not a reported value and is inconsistent with the graph.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Narrower slits produce wider central diffraction maxima.

questions.stem: Which relation best describes the first minimum for single-slit diffraction?

choices.A: nλ = 2d sin θ for Bragg reflection

explanations.B: Correct. For the first minimum of single-slit diffraction, a sin θ = λ. This relation explains why smaller slit width a produces a larger diffraction angle.
explanations.A: Bragg reflection applies to crystal planes, not the single slit described.
explanations.C: E = hf relates photon energy to frequency but does not locate diffraction minima.
explanations.D: P = ρgh describes hydrostatic pressure, not diffraction.

questions.stem: Why does reducing slit width increase the distance to the first minimum?

choices.A: A smaller slit width increases the diffraction angle
choices.B: A smaller slit width increases the laser wavelength
choices.C: A smaller slit width shortens the screen distance
choices.D: A smaller slit width eliminates interference

explanations.A: Correct. Since sin θ ≈ λ/a, decreasing a increases θ. A larger angle shifts the first minimum farther from the center on the screen.
explanations.B: Wavelength is a property of the laser and does not increase because the slit narrows.
explanations.C: The screen distance is fixed at 2.0 m in the passage.
explanations.D: Diffraction arises from wave interference; it is not eliminated by narrowing the slit.

questions.stem: What would happen if the screen were moved farther away while slit width stayed constant?

choices.D: The linear distance to the first minimum would increase
choices.A: The angular position would double automatically

explanations.D: Correct. The diffraction angle is set by wavelength and slit width, but the linear distance on the screen increases with screen distance.
explanations.A: The angular position is not set by screen distance; it is set by a and λ.
explanations.B: Moving the screen does not change laser wavelength.
explanations.C: The central maximum remains; it is simply projected over a larger distance.

questions.stem: Using x ≈ Lλ/a, what is x for L = 2.0 m, λ = 600 nm, and a = 0.20 mm?

explanations.C: Correct. Substituting gives x = 2.0 × 600×10⁻⁹ / 2.0×10⁻⁴ = 6×10⁻³ m = 6 mm. This matches the figure.
explanations.A: 0.6 mm is too small by a factor of 10.
explanations.B: 60 mm is too large by a factor of 10.
explanations.D: 600 mm is too large by a factor of 100.
